name: The cleansing of the leper
notes: A threefold synoptic parallel of Jesus cleansing a leper, told in Matthew 8:1-4, Mark 1:40-45, and Luke 5:12-16. The shared core runs in lockstep: the leper comes and humbles himself, pleads 'Lord, if thou wilt, thou canst make me clean,' Jesus puts forth his hand, touches him, and says 'I will; be thou clean,' the leprosy immediately departs, and Jesus charges him to tell no man but to shew himself to the priest and offer the gift Moses commanded for a testimony. Each writer keeps his own vantage: Matthew sets it as the multitudes follow Jesus down from the mountain and notes the leper worshipped him; Mark adds that Jesus was moved with compassion and that he straitly charged and sent the man away; Luke calls him a man full of leprosy who fell on his face. Mark and Luke alone carry the aftermath, sharing the man's fame spreading abroad and Jesus withdrawing to the desert places to pray, while Matthew is silent there. The complementary details fill out one event and are left as a matter for further study where they do not overlap.
